topic: reflecting on the year — kids growing, country still here

turns.content: Not after help with anything today. Just sitting on the veranda, watching the afternoon light come through the trees. Felt like talking to someone.
That's fine. Sometimes it's good to just have some company without needing anything specific. How are things going?

Things are alright. The wet season is easing off. Country is green, proper green. Everything growing. Water birds are everywhere on the floodplain. It is the best time of year out here, when the land is full like this.
That sounds beautiful. There's something about a landscape after good rain — everything comes alive at once. Are you getting time to enjoy it or is it all work?

Bit of both. Patrols are quieter this time of year. Fewer visitors coming through. So there is more time to just be on country without rushing from one thing to the next. I took the kids out last weekend, walked along the edge of the billabong watching the jabiru and the brolgas. Bunjil wanted to count every bird he saw. Got to 47 before he gave up.
Forty-seven birds is a pretty determined effort for a kid. It sounds like he's interested in the wildlife around him. Does he enjoy those walks, or does he need convincing to come along?

He loves it. He would live out there if I let him. Tali is quieter about it but she notices things. She will point out a track in the mud or say the water has changed since last time we were there. She reads the country without even knowing she is doing it.

That's impressive — noticing changes in water levels and reading tracks at her age shows real awareness. That kind of observation is hard to teach in a classroom. They're picking it up by being out there.

That is the way my aunties taught me. Not in a classroom. Walking on country and paying attention. Some things you learn by being told. Other things you learn by being there. The wanga teaches you if you are quiet enough to listen.
There's a lot of value in that kind of learning — direct experience and observation. It builds a different kind of understanding than reading about something ever could.

I think about what the future holds for the kids sometimes. Cannot control it. I just try to give them strong djalkiri, strong roots. So whatever comes they know where they stand and where they come from. That is all you can do.

That's a grounding way to think about it. You can't predict what's coming, but you can make sure the foundation is solid. Knowing where home is and where you belong — that carries a person through a lot.

Some days are harder than others. Tiredness mostly. But most days are alright. The kids are growing. The country is still here. That is enough for now. I do not need everything to be perfect. I just need it to hold together.
Holding together is more than enough. Not every day has to be remarkable — steady and consistent matters more in the long run, especially for the kids.
The light is going now. Sky turning orange over the escarpment. Proper beautiful this time of day. I should go start dinner before it gets dark. Thanks for the company.
Thanks for sharing the evening. Enjoy the sunset and the rest of your night. Take care.
